speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, there was a story about Lou and Perry from Colorado. There was a story about Lou and Perry from Colorado, about a cat in a stove.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me that story?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, you know, that snow has been pretty deep and the cold, you know, and finally I got so that they could get outside their shutdown for three days in the bed because the water was so deep in the house that they couldn't get out. And they left a cat in and then she jumped off and over on the oven door and went into the leg down in the oven. Well, when they come in, they shut the oven door and never thought about it and built up a fire and then they roasted the cat in.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So they burnt the feet off from her and they had to kill the cat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they find out the cat was in there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Because it got a stick in the bad cabin and they would go to look all over and see what was burning up and it was the cat that they roasted the hair off from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come it was flooding? Did it flood here much? Huh? Did it flood much in those days?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, in our house, the house stood down there on the flat. By God, the water come in the house so it went right up again the mattress in the bed.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, I've seen that flat in there after it was cleared up, a regular skating rink.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, you could skate all over that mattress.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would it flood for?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, several days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What month? What month would be the high month?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, it would generally be along in March when the snow started going bad and it started raining and snow up here six feet deep and the mud got full of water and started breaking through. By God, there would be water all over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How high above the creek was your place?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, it was just put right down the level of the bank.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: It was there for three years and then moved up on the hillside here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you move it? Huh? Why did your father move it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Because it was too wet down there.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: All the neighbors got together and tore the house down and moved it and set it up again in one day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everybody just pitched in help like that?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You're damn right. And by God, there were neighbors two days. It ain't like it is now.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And by God, put in here every Saturday night they'd all get together and they'd have a good time.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And when they'd meet on the road, they'd always stop and talk. Now, when you meet your neighbor, they just drive faster and look the other way.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Neighbors are called anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you exchange work a lot with your neighbors?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, quite a little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I did that because I was too young to begin with. But, well, I've had this place since 1911.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So I've been here quite a while too.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And of course,

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: the folks moved on the first 1891.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These winners, were there heavy snow winners that you remember?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes. Hell, there was nothing to see six and seven feet of snow here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: By God, Boper had nine. Elkhiver had 13 feet of snow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get by when the snow was so deep?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, mostly stay home and if you wanted to go on your horse, it was mostly skis or web shoes.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, all the... Boudinie all got a few sheaves and they cut the wool off from them and spin and make join. And the women, they'd never go to a neighbor unless there'd be a knitting socks or something.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: When they knit, there was five needles in the knitting needles for... To knit on, there was one extra that they thread the loops on and over on the main needle.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And the Americans, they generally used a crochet hook and crocheted sweaters for the kids.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Now, I've seen good many hard times and I hope some of these kids now, they'll never see anything like it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But I've lived through it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the cabin that Joe Wells had?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, it was just a little old cabin.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: With the bicon, it was... I'm now excited with the little awful little I'm being said.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: The Indian kids, I wasn't a bit afraid of them, but the nigger kids, they were too damn polite. I was a little spooky of them, but they got damn good friends of mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Roy and Chuck used to play pranks a lot?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Roy, yeah, Chuck, he used to play the harp, mouse harp. And boy, he liked to dance too.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And that was from an egg. Well,

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: the white girls around here, they wasn't a better master. They'd dance with them nigger boys just as well as anybody else. And boy, we'd just as soon dance with them nigger girls as any other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of music did he play with his mouth harp?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, it was waltzes and cadreels and stuff like that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He played at the dances.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, and then the loins just come in and they all... there was four boys and they all played the fiddle a little and the girls had played the banjo and the guitar.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh heck, I'll tell you, it was lots of fun too, and by god, they were all nervous.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you dance in the schoolhouse? Is that where you'd dance?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, there were a good many dancers in the old nigger schoolhouse. And old Joe Wells, he couldn't dance, but boy, he'd get up and dance a jig.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Especially when they're starting playing old black Joe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, is that true?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Joe a pretty strong man?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, he was a pretty husky nigger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do they call that his schoolhouse?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Wells was built on his place, on the corner of his place, so they called it the Wells Schoolhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who built it, do you know?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, all the neighbors got together and cut the logs and built it and Joe had a single horse and they used that one horse to roll the logs up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he gave him the land? He gave him the land?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, he gave him the chance to bed on the land.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And when they got to their schoolhouse, then Joe's daughter lived in the old schoolhouse for years. And there's one thing I'm mad about, that then god damn bastards had to tear that schoolhouse down and burn it up. They should have left it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So people could have seen what went on in olden days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's where you went to school, right?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend much time with Joe, around Joe?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, you know, he had three kids, was together with the kids.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They had one dog up there that got to rabies and they didn't have to get out. And I was over at Andrew's, I was pretty well grown up and nigga Mary come over there and the dog had the rabies, she sneaked out through the back door. And they had a gun down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I went down, I killed the dog, all right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she a good cook?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: None better.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: She had been a cook for the Welsh boys while she was a young girl. While she was a slave girl. By god no, she could cook just as good as any woman in the country.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: She'd been a slave back in Carolina, just like Joe.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: She took that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well she was Joe's wife.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So when the Welsh boys come out they brought him. And the Welsh boys, you know on this side of Moscow there is a deep cut there and there is some buildings right across the cut there. There is where the Welsh boys died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did the Welsh boys treat Joe like a brother?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You're damn right they did.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And they even helped Joe build the cabin up here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Then Joe's kids, hell they were down at the Welsh boys half of the time.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And Frank Welsh was a good blacksmith. He done the blacksmithing all the people up in here and come was a good cook. And boy you never could come down there if it was around meal time unless you had to come in and have dinner with him.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Frank had little black mustache and come was always smooth shaved. But he was lighter color.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear that Old Man Wells back in Carolina had given Joe money just like he gave Frank and Crom money to come out here?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, but they took Joe right along with him. So they all come in the same bunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people stop and eat at the Wells place?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: By God, everybody went up and down the road stopping at them. Even Boville had a good many meals at Joe Wells.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would there be a lot of people there for a meal? Do you think there would be more than a couple of people there for a meal? Would a whole bunch of people eat there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh hell, sometimes they'd have a house full.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And Joe, you know, he liked to drink. Well they'd stop and feed their horses. And he'd always have a bottle down in the barn and give them teamsters a drink. And boy, they always was willing to stop there.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And hell, whiskey, you know, they would buy it for $3 a gallon those days.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And now, my God, $5, $6 for a fifth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of Joe getting into trouble with Marshall Hayes and Troy?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, he got in trouble every once in a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you know, with Hayes you mean?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah. Yes, and by God, Andrew Olson was there and he took Joe Wells' part. And by God, Hayes went out through the door head first.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then Joe happened to be married to my older sister.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I heard Joe used to go to Troy with some of the other boys from around here and they'd have a good time.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: The damn right they'd have a good time. Joe wasn't a damn bit stingy neither.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: By God, he'd treat just as fast as any of them. And Chuck was the same way.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But Troy, he wasn't much to drink. He kept pretty sober all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did Joe make a living?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, hell, as quick as the starting loggin'. Hell, he had some of the best horses in the country and he logged. By God, now he took in some real logs. Oh, boy, they were good loggers and by God, he had a couple Irishmen that was working for him and by God, they wouldn't work for nobody else but Joe Wells.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you saying that Joe was a real hard worker?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: The damn right old Joe would work.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Chuck wasn't a... he'd work but you know he had diabetical options. So he had to be by his one leg and he had to keep that pack was cuttin' all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did Joe log? Did he have an operation for logging?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, you know, anybody that had logs to sell to the company, he'd take the job and haul the log in. And he'd get so much to sell and put logs in him and the owners that owned the log would get so much.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, old Joe was alright.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I've heard that Lou used to have sheep.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, yeah. Yes, they had sheep and she had a great big high -spinning wheel. I think it must have been damn close to seven feet tall. And she'd turn nuts with her hand and spin yarn and then she'd knit German socks for the men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: German socks? What's that?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, they were big high socks that would go clean up to the knee.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Had a joint in German socks or felt boots, neither one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What boots? What kind of boots?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Felt boots. They were heavy -filled and by God they were about name -shake. And that leached right up to the knee.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I've seen them and I wore them too. Hmm.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So, you're telling me about Joe that nobody cared whether he was black or not. Nobody cared. Everybody liked him anyway.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Damn right.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No one from the built to Lutheran church, by God, old Joe, put in his church or built that too.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, the first Christmas, they had a Christmas tree down there. And the nigger kids all over the side, Joe, and they all got a bag of candy. And boy, them nigger kids were tickled.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I guess maybe they never had a bag of candy.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, when a fellow sits and thinks back, that pudding of air makes the fellow cry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father have something to do with the first church being built?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, he is the one that got the plot for the church and he bagged quite a lot of money for it. And by God, the fellow that had the place that the church was built on, he gave them the timber. And they logged the timber down to the mill and Pete Dahlbergs sold it out for lumber. And if they furnished a man, he furnished a mill to sell out to lumber.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, they got short of money and I and this old Martin Olson, we split the shingles for the north side of the church. Martin split them and I shaved them for the north side of the church.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And it was all volunteer work.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And now, it's all been volunteer work. Well, there's a bunch of Californians come in. By God, they even tried to show us how to walk on the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You don't think they're doing much for the country, huh?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, you're damn right. This neighbor over here, he tried to fence in the road too. And just leave a little street here, ten feet wide. But by God, I've seen too that the sheriff come out and had a pretty good long talk with him. So he had to move back his fences.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Nils Lund, the miner? Do you remember that foolish miner name of Nils Lund?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He's over on Boulder Creek.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, you bet I remember. Nils and his brother Martin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, they were pretty heavy fellows. They were Swedes.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I thought Nils was sort of a crazy guy.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, that one of them was. Nils was kind of crazy. He went to go back to Sweden and he died at sea. And I guess he was buried right in the ocean. Because it took seventeen days to cross the ocean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was Nils, what was it about Nils that was funny? What was it about this fellow that he was sort of a funny guy?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, he wasn't all that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He always says there's bad news North America.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He meant this crazy North America, this country here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, he didn't like it here at all. He was going back to Sweden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said this country was crazy?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever tell you why?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No. But he'd get out and work every summer so he always had a little money so he could get along.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He had a homestead over there. But then the monkeys with him, you know, over in Moscow and tried to put him in the asylum and he never would want to go back to Moscow and prove up. So he sold the homestead for fifty dollars and then he was going back to Sweden.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You know, some of these people, the worst damn thing they can do is to monkey with half crazy people.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Because if you leave them alone, you can get along pretty good with them. But if you monkey with them, you'll just make them that much worse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it about him that made people think he wasn't all there? What did he do that made people think he was half crazy?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, you know, he wouldn't socialize with anybody about the only damn place. It'd be he'd stop here once in a while. Because Dad was one of these fellows, you know, he could get along with anybody.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: If they wanted to say something, my God, Dad would help them along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If they wanted to say something?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, if they were talking, I got Dad to help them ride along.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And Dad would have a melapin after a little bit.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Your dad, he just liked people.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, Dad could get along with anybody. And Mother was the same way.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's a story I heard about this Lund, this man. The story was that he used to mine. He said he would mine where a tree pointed. He thought God was showing him where he should dig his holes in the ground.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah. Yes, he believed in God alright.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And that's a whole lot more than a lot of them do.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And if you stop and think about it, there is some great power that handles this world too. It isn't a people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Arthur, why do you think that? Why do you say that? Why isn't it the people?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, the people think they know it all. But you take and go out and you do something wrong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you get kind of scared, don't you?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Just so nobody don't catch you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By gosh, it's your heart that told you that you have done something wrong and it kind of scares you a little, don't it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, by George, I fully believe there is some great overpower from somewhere.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But there is a lot of people that don't think that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, I was going to ask you if you remember Big Anderson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember him?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He was an old hermit that lived...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: His name was Big Anderson. He lived by Anderson Crossing and he was sort of a hermit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember him?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, we called him Big Anderson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He was a pretty heavy sort of a man.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And God, he called him the wild animal. That is the devil. That was the wild animal and the man above.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was God?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That was supposed to be God.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he talk about religion?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, not too much, a little. But you know, he had one of the nice response on Barrett.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then he laid down there by the creek and prospected and dug holes in the hills to find gold and rented the place.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That is something, you know, them prospectors, by gosh, if they seem like they have to be digging in the ground all the time, it does not make any difference how good the place they got. They leave that layer alone and rent it to somebody else.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: It is just a kind of a damn fever.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He did not do very good at prospecting.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No. Like them old prospectors, there was none of them that done anything.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Now there was an old fella up here. His name was Pete Jackson. He laid up there and prospected for years and he had two of the best farms in the Genesee country and had them rented away. And he spent all the money he got down there up there in the prospect hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he prospect?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Up on Ruby Creek.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I thought lots of times if my eyes were so I could see, I would like to go up there and see what that old prospect looked like today where he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see it in the old days?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That is the damn right I did.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And it is right where they call it the Jackson Butte right now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What? How did he do? He did not do very good either?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, I do not think he ever made a dollar out of that prospect. I think he spent every dollar he got up from the farm.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Sometimes he had a little fella by the name of Joe Emang to come up there and help him. And that Joe Emang, he went back to the old country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know, was he just using a placer? Just placer mining or was he doing more than that?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, hell, Christ, there was tunnels that were 100 feet long.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There is one tunnel right over across the creek here that was put in over 80 years ago. That tunnel is 127 feet long and I have been to the bottom of a circle.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Dyers usually go in there and hibernate during the winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know who dug the tunnel here? Do you know who dug this tunnel here?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: His name was Day.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There were three, four of them days. There was a Day day, an Irish day, and a Mark day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They live around here? Were they here in this country?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, one of them had a farm right down here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And, now, Irish Day, he had a cabin out there. And it wouldn't surprise me unless cabin still stands there and there is a logging road right over the tunnel. Now,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: where is that tunnel from here?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: It's just about straight south of here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: About three, four miles. But, of course, there is no road across there, just wild country. Hell, the river is right down below us. Hell, I've caught lots of fish in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Big ones?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, I've caught some pretty good. Hell, I've caught salmon in there. 14, 15 pounders.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There was one spring, I caught 11 salmon down there. And the smallest one was three pound. From that to 15.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Henry Benson told me he used to go fishing with you down there.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Henry, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is Henry still alive?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He sure is.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He lives in Onaway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's pretty fat, isn't he?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, no, I wouldn't say he's fat. He's pretty old. He's in his 80s.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, he's born right up here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He told me he used to pal around with you.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah, we're hundreds quite a little together, too.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I think the last time I and Henry hunted together, he jumped out a big buck. That hog dressed 256 pounds.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And I sent Henry home with half of that deer.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He was breaking, well, his brother was breaking on the railroad up here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: George is dead. George Benson and Nina, his older sister, I think she lives in Paloo. That's right.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I saw her, too. She was there with his house when I stopped by.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Arthur, didn't you haul logs for the Boville Hotel when they were building it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, I hauled the logs up to the Benson's sawmill, up to the Dahlberg's sawmill, and then from the Sordid Lumbria, we hauled the lumber to Boville. And by God, mud came to the hubs and the wagons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Boville look like then?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, he was a pretty...

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, I mean the town, the place where the, the site of the town.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, there was only one house up there, the old Warren Hub.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And that old Warren cabin still stands there and that's over a hundred years old.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There were three brothers of them, Warrens, Lewis Frank and Ellis.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, I can't think of the third one's name. I knew all of them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They were the first ones in that country.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah. They're homesteading and up there.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Then, when Warrens come, he bought them out and he come from Texas. And he was a cowboy. And they shipped the cattle in from Texas to stop the ranshipping.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this Boville? Who are you talking about? Boville or Warren?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Boville. Oh, yeah. Huh.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He married a widow. She was the one that had the money.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then, when they get up here, they had twin girls. And I think one of them, the lady down here on the coast, some of them, maybe both of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a guy was Boville? What was he like? Was he an aristocrat?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, he was a pretty good sized man, dark, complacent, kind of long mustache.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's a lot of it. Many are general Bovilles. When we hauled lumber up there, it was always the hired men that unload the lumber and we'd go to dinner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the hotel like? What was it like inside? Was it nice?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, to begin with, it was just a little log cabin. Then, they built a hotel up there and that got pretty good, a butcher shop. And hauled many beef up there and sold to the fellow veterans, the butcher shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He built the hotel out of the lumber that you brought up there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You bet we hauled the lumber for that hotel.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: If I could see, and one thing I'd like to see them girls again now.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: One of them was up at Boville here four or five years ago and visited.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I think she had a car wreck and had a leg hurt pretty badly. So, I guess she's kind of a cripple.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I think you're right. I think you're right.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I know damn well I am.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the place like before the lumber company came in?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, it was timber all around it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And by God, it was a good fishing stream too. Hell, it was nothing to catch trout that long.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And the same way all the way up the river here. Shoot the biggest catch on the creek down here. 186 trout in one day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of Boville? What did I think of it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: By God, old Boville was a damn nice man.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And by God, when you brought a load of lumber up there, by God, you got the cash right now. You didn't have to wait for it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He paid for the whole of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Arthur, did you hear about the Agnes Leener killing? Did you hear about Agnes Leener getting killed?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, I do. And by God, I can put my fingers on her grave too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, that damn just gentleman, I have an idea, got her into the tunnel and raped her. And I suppose she put up a fight and then he just took and killed her.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: She's buried on the same lot that my neighbor, my notion is buried on down here on the Lutheran Cemetery.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, I knew Agnes Leener swung her quite a few times on the third floor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she a pretty girl?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, she was a nice girl.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I wonder why he bothered her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he known her for a long time?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, that damn damn outfit was just kind of an outlaw outfit anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, after he killed her, did he shoot himself too or what happened?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, he shot himself and it wasn't his gun either. It was her brother's gun he used. And there was only one shell left in the gun. So he killed himself and he didn't make it so he didn't die before the next day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do? Did they drag him out of there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, they took him out and I guess they took him into Volvo to the hospital and he died there. And he is buried on that poor man's graveyard in Moscow, right back to the main graveyard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He used five bullets before he got to himself?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, there was only a couple of three bullets in the gun, see. And he didn't have any more.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I wonder if he thought he was going to get away with it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, see, he shot her and money shot one shot at her brother but he missed him. Money used to last one on himself and the gun went empty.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Somebody told me that when people got him they dragged him out of there. They just didn't care whether they killed him or not anymore.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, they didn't.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No man's gentleman says, oh, he got away with everything. He'll get out of that too but he didn't. I knew his dad well. He was road boss here for a good many years. I worked good many days for his dad.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I hope he was a better guy than the son was.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, yeah. He was a good man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he, did old man Dillman have a bear that he took around the country?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, yeah, he used to trap bear all over. And then he'd get out and sell bear meat and if he could trade bear meat to some of the people around the country and get a dozen eggs, he'd one of the soft boil that eggs, then he'd sit down and eat a dozen of eggs to the meat.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And he leapt right on by. And I think I knew the girl too. Because we used to have a place right next to our school house and put near all the school moms that'd leave, that'd ride horse back, come into school in the morning and they'd leave the horse in our barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you know this, hello, Commissioner over at Moscow?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Ingle. Ingle.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I think Ingle, Jerry Ingle.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, hell, I knew his mother well before she was married.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, I've swung her in the walls a time or two too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did you hear about this teacher getting killed?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, that's hard to say. You know there was, well, on that grade down there, shoot, there was several girls that was killed down there. And there was one from California that disappeared that they never did find. Hell, them girls got killed in a throat cut. And they claimed it was some of the university students from Pullman that done it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's over on the Big Bear grade going down towards Kendrick?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, it's on that other grade down there going down to the Clearwater.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, I've been down that grade two, three times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where does the road go from to get to the Clearwater?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: It's on the other side of Pullman. Oh. Instead of coming to Moscow, we go the other way down there. Yeah. Yes, I've been over that grade a couple, three times too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, there was one story I heard that you, there's one story, didn't Sam Samowich shoot a guy?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes. And the other fella tried to kill him with an axe. And he caught Sam's shoulder, so it just hung. And Sam had his gun loaded out and T .B. Jones had it and T .B. Jones got the gun back. And Sam got the gun out and he says, I'll shoot him once and the steelkeeper come in. I'll shoot him twice and the steelkeeper come in. I'll shoot him three times, but then he goes down like a sober man. Hell, I knew Sam Samowich just about as well as I knew my brother. The camp was right over there and he was over there and after he got so he could get around, he'd sit around in the evening and patch watches with a bow and when they'd stop, he held Sam Samowich with a damn knife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how did that fight start?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, Sam was a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Foreman?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Section foreman and he fired this fella, well this fella says, He can't, you can't either and he was going to kill him with an axe. That's how it started and that started right in the country road. The railroad crossed the country road down here at Bercow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Sam have the gun on him right at the time?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, T .P. Jones had just brought the gun back and handed it to him.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well did T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: hand him the gun after he got axed or before?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Before he killed him. Well you know T .P. brought back the gun that morning after he went back to work. Because it was quite a lot of bow hunt trouble at that time and put near all the bosses carried the gun. And T .P. didn't have one so he borrowed Sam's so he could get one of his own. And when he got one of his own he got Sam's gun back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Sam kill this guy when he shot him?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, Sam killed him already.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And I think that fella was buried right up there in the Bowville cemetery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why were they having trouble? Why were they carrying guns, the foremen?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well hell, there was always some of them coming around causing trouble in camp. And when they'd get one of them fellas coming around making trouble they'd run them out of the country.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: The camp was right over here, I've seen a lot of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that I .W .W.?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, I .W .N.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They called it I Won't Work. Another said I Won't Work. They all spelled I .W .N. see, it all spelled the same.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the I .W .W.'s that were causing the trouble?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Were they the reason they... No, they were causing trouble. One of them bastards come along you know and just sticks their head in the bunkhouse. They had trouble on boys and not damaging like every damn strike on boys and next morning there wouldn't be a man that would go to work. That's the way it went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear that the I .W .W.'s were the ones that got better conditions in the camp?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well,

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: oh they got... I don't think they got any better food but they got maybe a little better pay.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: did the Potlatch Lumber Company, did they start the town at Derry or did somebody, did McGowan and Henry do it by themselves?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, McGowan, I think bought that land from Braylock and that stuff and then of course you got permission from the railroad company and the railroad company put in a depot. And they surveyed the town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did McGowan work for Potlatch?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, they kind of worked for themselves. Oh yeah, they had offices right here in Potlatch, in Derry.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, I pointed out some of the government corners for them too.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I knew putting the government corner in this country because I done a lot of hunting and every damn time I'd find a government corner I'd pay attention to it. And by God it was one thing, if I ever went to a government corner I could always go back.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And I wouldn't need no compass neither. You take the timber and it's just as good as a compass. You look at a tree and by God you can tell who knows it himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, so you know all the big limbs is on the south side of the tree and the bark is darker on the north side, lighter on the north side of the tree than it is on the south side. And twelve o 'clock at any time the sun, as you can see, is a straight sun.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's a lot of people that don't know that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, if I ever went to a place I could go back to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you ever get lost in these woods?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Not for very long. Unless it was a hell of a snowstorm that was twisting the trees around so you couldn't see. And snow would blow until you couldn't open your eyes. By now being turned around. But if you sit down, if you get turned down and just pull the coat off your head and sit down a while, by God pretty soon the world will turn right around and you'll come straight again. There's a lot of people that don't know that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: By God, some people when they get good and lost, by God even if they get into your own house they don't know their own home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: It's true.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: The damn rabbit's true because I seen it happen to my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When I brought her in she says, what place is this?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And she was riding home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How had she gotten lost?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I was picking up her birds and got turned around until I was a day off going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She just didn't know which way she was going at all?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, she didn't have no idea where she was going.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Dad got lost one night cloudy night right in the woodshed and he couldn't find his way out of the woodshed.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Lots of things have happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find your mother when she was lost?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You bet I did. I could track putting you as good as a dog and I had a dog that was a damn side better. I put him on the track and by God he found it. And I was right behind of him too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long had she been missing?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yes, a couple of hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you give the dog some of her clothes to smell?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, I told the dog to go find him and by God he took right off.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And by God I was right behind of him too. And those days I could travel putting her like a razor.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I had no trouble but me to take a 35 mile blanket roll. After working all day and start from Tennessee and walk in here next morning at a 30 mile hike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just go all night?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That must have been hard.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: It was and generally no surface road and the dust always shot horses. You would just dust that deep in the road. Every four or five miles you would have to stop and take your shoes off and shake the dirt out of your shoes to be able to walk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would go a foot all the way?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You are damn right I would walk all the way.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I know one time I started from down there in Tennessee about five o 'clock in the evening and I walked in here and the folks were sitting down to breakfast.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: When Potlatch came in here and started buying the timber I heard they bought the timber very very cheap.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well hell, all the timber they bought was the time and they bought that for a dollar a thousand. And then they just counted the trees and then trees had to be 16 inches or through or bigger at this jump. Well all the fur and time right they didn't sell that at all. And when they got the timber deed they got a deed for the timber in such a section. And fur and time right, white fur and sprues. And time right that was all they gave. They got that for nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So which were the species that they wanted? What were the kinds of trees they counted in the survey? What kinds of trees did they count in the survey?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Just yellow pine and white pine. Black pine and all the rest of it wasn't counted at all. Then when they got, bought it, the state gave them the timber deed, the state gave them the rest of the timber.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And of course the farmers that had homesteads used the same darn thing.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: The potlucks didn't have warehouses, what it was called then, didn't buy I don't think more than a tenth of the timber and pay for it. The rest of it was all they gave.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So when you were out with Bill Helmer the only timber he cruised was the white pine and the yellow pine. That's all he counted.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That's all they counted because there, well he was more to open up the lines and take out the railroad squares. The log, he wasn't a cruising much unless there was somebody that wanted to sell a piece of land then he'd cruise it for them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And they'd walk it the same way and buy them the Milwaukee cruisers.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They'd cruise it and by God you could sell your timber right at home and you'd get a check right at home and then you'd do the rest of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a pretty good deal for the homesteaders or did they get rooked?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, the homesteaders didn't get much out of it. It was the company that was getting all the drag out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All the what?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: It was the company that was getting all the property.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you sell timber to them, your father? Or you sell timber?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, my father sold the timber on the place here and then by God it was ten years that it stood here that we couldn't do a damn thing.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Unless on best 40 he saved the black pine on that so you could cut out the black pine and make wood.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: What you're saying is that Potlatch didn't help the people so much as they helped themselves.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They helped themselves a whole lot more than helped the people. Of course, it got so the people could get work all right and camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what it was like snowshoeing with Bill Helmer or snowshoeing in the winter?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Damn right I remember what it was like and I walked a good many hundred miles on snowshoes.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Tell me how that was done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you do it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, them snowshoes. Well, there was long ones. Them were the trampers. But the barefos, they were just kind of three -cornered. You could walk and turn around them. That's what we always used in the woods. Hell, I had a set of snowshoes till the old buildings went up and they went up. Hell, I snowshoes a lot of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, could you use the same trail over again? Could you use the same trail again?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, yes.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, use the same trail over and over.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then we had the skis. I used to ski a lot. Yet it was nothing for me to make fifty and sixty foot jumps on the steep hillside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see that mountain up there? Which direction?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: The Ture Butte. Oh yeah.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: We'd ski from the top of that plane to the bottom on skis in the early days.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: In the early days, by gosh, I have a mess. No, got deep. We'd ski down here on the brakes and kettle a deer every once in a while. But now we run short of something to eat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you just camp out in the winter? You just camp out down there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, we'd go down in the morning and come back at night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And kill a deer for dinner?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Why, we'd kill a deer and then make a pike sack out of the deer and come back at night, come home at night. I've carried quite a few deer on my back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was your lower place? The place where you wintered? On the ridge?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where the steel schoolhouse used to be?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I don't think so.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, that steel schoolhouse was on our place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where? Now whereabouts would that be now?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where Waja Lake is?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah. Well, it'd be north and east of Waja Lake.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where the Four Corners is down there? And you know where the churches down there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I think I do, I'm not sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's where? In there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, there was two churches down there. The Lutheran and then there was the American church.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And it wasn't too low.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know where Ed Hall's at?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah. The Lutheran church, I think, was right at the corner of the place where Ed Hall's at.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you weren't too far from his place? You were pretty close to his place?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, but further north. A couple of miles further north.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you thinking of Atwater Lake? Huh? Are you thinking of Atwater Lake?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, do you remember what instruments you used to survey with Bill Helmer?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, I compass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to school? What grade did you go up to?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, to tell the truth, the way it is now, I don't think it'd be more than about the third grade now.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Because, hell, if I go to school, the best was two and three months. Well, what you learnt one time, you forgot to the next one.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So what little I know, I've studied that mission.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you stopped going to school?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, about fourteen, fifteen. Oh,

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I was going to ask you a little more about the herding of cattle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you, what was the grass like that the cattle grazed on? Huh? What was the grass like that the cattle grazed on?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, it was just common pine grass. It's the same kind of grass as it is now. But those days, it wasn't worth it that damn grass would get that high.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Maybe two feet.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: By God, there was, and the Indians kept it burnt off, there was pressure down here between the big timber that you could take a mowing machine and a team of horses and mow grass in among the big timber and rake it up. Now, by God, you can't walk in among your big timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you sure the Indians burnt the grass here?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, either the Indians or the lightning. It was nothing those days to have several lightning storms during the summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you think the Indians did it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, one day I was up here, one day leaving the fall, they weren't particularly about the fire. Hell, I know they used to camp up here. When they'd leave, hell, there'd always be a smoke behind them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they burnt the country to make better grazing for their horses?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, easier hunting, getting around.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And you know, the Indians, the bucks, they didn't do anything. They'd go out and hunt, but the damn squirrels, they'd be the ones that'd have to pack in the meat and take care of the hives and make demarcations and all that. And try the meat. That was the squirrels' work. The bucks wouldn't do that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And by God, from the white starting coming in here, there's a damn good many half -bridge and quarter -bridge right around here now. Even some of my neighbors had got quite a lot of Indian blood in them. Because when she'd move in with a white man, she didn't have to work so hard because he'd bring the meat in and dress that. She wouldn't have to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was easier for her to live with a white man, you say? So it was easier to live with a white?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes. Hell, right. And mostly Frenchmen that marriage caused.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I didn't know there were Frenchmen in here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, there was quite a few Frenchmen in here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's several of the neighbor women right here that's got Indian blood in them and quite a little too.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And one of my friends is married to a full -blooded squirrel. She's around 90 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did those marriages work out pretty good usually with an Indian and white?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: My God, they got along good. They got along at times better than a lot of the whites did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So marriages didn't always work out in the old days either, huh?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's often they don't work out now.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh hell.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, you know, in the late 19th century, her mother and father were always the fighters and whipping the kids.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, it would be kind of fun to, if I could hear and see, to see Nana again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come you never got married?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Too damn many people had too many things to say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Too damn many people had too damn many lies to tell.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And there were a lot of things that they didn't know a damn thing about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know how that is?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: A lot of people got to tell something about somebody that they don't know a damn thing about. And it would be a great big long lie.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I'm not sure I know why, what that means. I don't know exactly what you're saying about why that means you didn't get married yourself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People trying to push you into it, is that it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, they just told the girls that I went with too damn many lies.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I don't think I would have had very much trouble to have gotten hang of ancient years stage right here and there.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well there's lots of guys, lots of old timers that are bachelors.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, there was quite a few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what were you going to say? Huh? Were you going to say something?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that the whites ruined the country, the Indians took good care of it, what do you mean?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How's that?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You told me that the whites ruined the country and the Indians took good care of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well the whites come in here and they ruined all the timber and all the berries and all the fish and if that ain't ruined it, I'd like to know what was.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
